name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayah

```arabic
وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ ٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِـَٔادَمَ فَسَجَدُوٓا۟ إِلَّآ إِبْلِيسَ أَبَىٰ وَٱسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٣٤)
```

```english
And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam,’ they prostrated, but not Iblis: he refused and acted arrogantly, and he was one of the faithless. (34)
```

## (Commentary)

You have already realized that His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ** (and what you used to conceal), indicates the occurrence of a concealed matter that became manifest after having been hidden. This is not without a connection to His saying: **أَبَىٰ وَاسْتَكْبَرَ وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ** (he refused and acted arrogantly, and he was one of the disbelievers), where He did not simply say "he refused, acted arrogantly, and disbelieved." You also realized that the story of the prostration (السجدة) is situated like an event—or is indeed an event—occurring between His saying, the Exalted: **إِنِّي أَعْلَمُ مَا لَا تَعْلَمُونَ** (Indeed I know what you do not know), and His saying: **وَأَعْلَمُ مَا تُبْدُونَ وَمَا كُنتُمْ تَكْتُمُونَ** (and I know what you reveal and what you used to conceal). Thus, His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ** (And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate before Adam’), is like a sentence extracted from between other sentences to transition through it to the story of the Garden (الجنة). For these verses, as you have learned, were only brought forth to explain the nature of human vicegerency (خلافة الإنسان), its position, the manner of his descent to the world, and what his affair leads to in terms of felicity (سعادة) and wretchedness (شقاء). Therefore, what matters regarding the story of the prostration here is only its summary that leads to the story of the Garden and the descent (هبوط) of Adam. This is the reason for shifting from elaboration (الإطناب) to conciseness (الإيجاز). Perhaps this is also the secret behind the transition (الإلتفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the first person (التكلم) in His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذْ قُلْنَا لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ اسْجُدُوا** (And when We said to the angels, ‘Prostrate’), after His saying: **وَإِذْ قَالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلَائِكَةِ إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ** (And when your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set...’). Based on what has passed, the attribution of concealment (الكتمان) to the angels—while it was the act of Iblis—is based on the linguistic convention of attributing the act of an individual to the group if he is mixed among them and not distinguished from them. Another possibility exists: that the apparent meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **إِنِّي جَاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً** (Indeed I am going to set a vicegerent on the earth), implies the absolute nature of vicegerency (إطلاق الخلافة) even over the angels, as supported by their being commanded a second time to prostrate. This would cause a thought to occur in the hearts of the angels, as they did not think an earthly being could prevail over everything, even over them. Some narrations point to this meaning, as will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **اسْجُدُوا لِآدَمَ** (Prostrate before Adam), implies the permissibility of prostrating to other than Allah in general, provided it is a greeting (تحية) and an honoring (تكرمة) of the other, and it contains submission to Allah, the Exalted, by complying with His command. Its parallel is His saying, the Exalted, in the story of Yusuf (peace be upon him): **وَرَفَعَ أَبَوَيْهِ عَلَى الْعَرْشِ وَخَرُّوا لَهُ سُجَّدًا ۖ وَقَالَ يَا أَبَتِ هَٰذَا تَأْوِيلُ رُؤْيَايَ مِن قَبْلُ قَدْ جَعَلَهَا رَبِّي حَقًّا** — "And he seated his parents high upon the throne, and they fell down prostrate before him. He said, ‘Father, this is the fulfillment of my dream of long ago, which my Lord has made come true.’" (Yusuf: 100). The summary of the matter is that you have already learned in *Surat al-Fatihah* that worship (العبادة) — *the act of the servant placing himself in the station of servitude and performing that which establishes and confirms it* — means that the act of worship must possess the capacity to manifest the Lordship (المولوية) of the Master or the servitude (العبدية) of the servant, such as prostration (السجدة), bowing (الركوع), standing (القيام) before him while he sits, and walking behind him while he walks, and so on. Whenever this aforementioned capacity increases, the act of worship becomes more defined as servitude. The clearest of acts in indicating the might of Lordship and the lowliness of servitude is prostration (السجدة), due to the falling upon the earth and placing the forehead upon it. As for what some have imagined—that prostration is an essential worship (عبادة ذاتية)—it is of no consequence, for that which is essential (الذاتي) does not vary or fail to occur. This very act can be performed by its doer with a motive other than veneration and worship, such as mockery and derision, in which case it would not be worship despite containing everything that the act of worship contains. Yes, the meaning of worship is clearer in prostration than in other acts. Since it is not an essential worship, it is not by its very essence (لذاته) exclusive to Allah, the Glorified, based on the premise that the One worshipped (المعبود) is restricted to Him, the Exalted. If there were a prohibition, it would be from the perspective of a religious (شرعي) or rational (عقلي) prohibition. That which is prohibited religiously or rationally is nothing other than attributing Lordship (الربوبية) to other than Him, the Exalted. As for greeting another or honoring them without attributing Lordship, but merely for the sake of social custom and greeting, there is no evidence for its prohibition. However, the religious taste (الذوق الديني) derived from intimacy with the outward aspects of religion dictates the exclusivity of this act to Him, the Exalted, and the prohibition of using it in other than His case, even if nothing but greeting and honoring is intended. As for the prohibition of everything that manifests sincerity to Allah by expressing love for His righteous servants or for the graves of His friends (أولياء) or their relics, it is something for which no rational or textual evidence exists at all. We shall return to the discussion of this subject in a suitable place, Allah, the Exalted, willing.

## (Discussion of Narrations)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "When Allah created Adam, He commanded the angels to prostrate to him. The angels said within themselves: 'We did not think that Allah created a creation more noble to Him than us, for we are His neighbors and we are the closest of creation to Him.' So Allah said: 'Did I not say to you that I know what you reveal and what you used to conceal?' regarding what they revealed of the matter of the children of the Jinn (بني الجان) and what they concealed within themselves. So the angels who said what they said sought refuge (لاذت) with the Throne (العرش)."

In the same *Tafsir*, narrated from Ali ibn al-Husayn (peace be upon him) regarding its meaning, it says: "When the angels realized they had fallen into a mistake, they sought refuge with the Throne; and they were a group of angels, those who were around the Throne, not all the angels..." until he said: "So they seek refuge around the Throne until the Day of Resurrection."

**I say:** The content of these two narrations can be inferred from His saying, recounting the words of the angels: **وَنَحْنُ نُسَبِّحُ بِحَمْدِكَ وَنُقَدِّسُ لَكَ** (while we celebrate Your praise and proclaim Your sanctity) until His saying: **سُبْحَانَكَ لَا عِلْمَ لَنَا إِلَّا مَا عَلَّمْتَنَا ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْعَلِيمُ الْحَكِيمُ** (Immaculate are You! We have no knowledge except what You have taught us. Indeed You are the All-knowing, the All-wise).

It will be mentioned later that the Throne (العرش) is knowledge (العلم), and narrations to this effect have been transmitted from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them), so understand that. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **وَكَانَ مِنَ الْكَافِرِينَ** (and he was one of the disbelievers), refers to the people of Iblis from among the Jinn (الجان) who were created before man. The Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنسَانَ مِن صَلْصَالٍ مِّنْ حَمَإٍ مَّسْنُونٍ وَالْجَانَّ خَلَقْنَاهُ مِن قَبْلُ مِن نَّارِ السَّمُومِ** — "Certainly We created man out of a dry clay [drawn] from an altered mud. And the jinn We created before from a smokeless fire." (al-Hijr: 26-27). According to this narration, the attribution of concealment (الكتمان) to all the angels does not require any special interpretation; rather, it is literal, for the concealed thought occurred in the hearts of all the angels. There is no contradiction between this narration and those indicating that the concealed matter was what Iblis was hiding of his refusal to submit to Adam and his arrogance if called to prostrate, for it is possible to derive both meanings, as is indeed the case.

In *Qisas al-Anbiya*, narrated from Abu Basir, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'Did the angels prostrate and place their foreheads on the ground?' He said: 'Yes, as an honoring (تكرمة) from Allah, the Exalted.'"

In *Tuhaf al-Uqul*, it is said: "The prostration of the angels to Adam was only an act of obedience to Allah and out of love from them for Adam."

In *al-Ihtijaj*, narrated from Musa ibn Ja'far, from his forefathers: "A Jewish man asked the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him) about the miracles of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in comparison to the miracles of the prophets. He said: 'This is Adam, for whom Allah made His angels prostrate; did He do anything like this for Muhammad?' Ali said: 'That was indeed so, but Allah made His angels prostrate to Adam, yet their prostration was not a prostration of obedience such that they worshipped Adam besides Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, but rather an acknowledgment of Adam's excellence and a mercy from Allah upon him. And Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was given what is superior to this: for Allah, the Majestic and Sublime, blessed him in His dominion (جبروت), as did all the angels, and the believers were commanded to seek blessings (الصلوة) upon him; so this is an increase for him, O Jew.'"

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: "Allah created Adam and he remained as a form for forty years. The accursed Iblis would pass by him and say: 'For what matter were you created?' He said: 'The Knower (العالم).' Iblis said: 'If Allah commands me to prostrate to this, I shall surely disobey Him...'" until he said: "Then Allah, the Exalted, said to the angels: 'Prostrate to Adam,' and they prostrated. Then Iblis brought out what was in his heart of envy (الحسد) and refused to prostrate."

In *al-Bihar*, from *Qisas al-Anbiya*, narrated from al-Sadiq (peace be upon him), who said: "Iblis was commanded to prostrate to Adam, and he said: 'O Lord, by Your Might, if You excuse me from prostrating to Adam, I shall surely worship You with a worship such as no one has ever worshipped You with the like of it.' Allah, Majestic is His Majesty, said: 'I love to be obeyed from where I intend.'" And he said: "Indeed, Iblis wailed four times: the first on the day he was cursed, the day he was cast down to the earth, the day Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was sent after a hiatus (فترة) of messengers, and when the Mother of the Book (أم الكتاب) was revealed. And he snorted twice: when Adam ate from the tree, and when he was cast down from the Garden." And he said regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْآتُهُمَا** (their nakedness became exposed to them): "Their nakedness was not seen, then it became visible and prominent." And he said: "The tree that Adam was forbidden from was the ear of grain (السنبلة)."

**I say:** In the narrations—which are numerous—there is support for what we have mentioned regarding the prostration (السجدة).
